Q1: I tried to keep a steady routine, usually studying in the evenings after my extracurricular activities. I'd set aside time to listen to lectures and recorded materials since I learn best by hearing things explained. With nine online courses, I spread my eighteen hours across the week, but honestly it was hard to give each course the attention it deserved. I kept a loose schedule but things often shifted around depending on what was due.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines and what else was going on in my life. I aimed for consistency, but with so many courses running simultaneously, I'd end up prioritizing whichever one had something due soonest. Some weeks I'd be really focused on one course and barely touch another. It wasn't ideal, but it's how I managed to keep up with everything.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I knew there was new content posted or when I needed to check on upcoming deadlines. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a discussion post or a peer had replied to something I wrote, and that would pull me back in. I also logged in just to watch videos since that's my main way of absorbing the material. It became almost automatic to check in most days.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks it became pretty normal to log in during my morning coffee or right after dinner. The discussion boards helped because I wanted to see if anyone had responded to my posts or if there were interesting conversations happening. Having peers to interact with made it feel less isolating. It just became part of my daily rhythm, like checking messages.
Q5: The video lectures were absolutely essential for me since I'm an auditory learner. I'd watch them multiple times, sometimes just listening while doing other things to let the concepts sink in. The discussion posts were also helpful because hearing other students' perspectives, even in written form, helped me process ideas differently. When instructors responded in discussions, that felt really valuable.
Q6: I'll admit I often skipped over the reading materials and PDFs. They just didn't stick with me the way audio content did. I'd download them intending to read later but they'd pile up. Some of the optional quizzes I also ignored because I was already stretched thin with assignments. It wasn't that they weren't useful, I just couldn't prioritize everything.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could actually feel myself understanding something better afterward. If I listened to a lecture and suddenly a concept clicked, that was satisfying. Discussions where people actually engaged thoughtfully also felt worthwhile. But busy work or activities that felt disconnected from the main content? Those felt like a waste when I was already struggling to keep up.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford most of the time. Required ones I'd force myself through even when I was exhausted or unmotivated. With optional stuff, I'd tell myself I'd get to it later, but later rarely came. There's a guilt that comes with skipping optional things because you know they could help, but you're just too drained to care.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of the audio lectures and listened to them on my phone while commuting or doing chores. I also used external YouTube videos to supplement topics I didn't fully grasp. Sometimes I'd record myself summarizing concepts and play it back. None of that showed up in the LMS, but it was a huge part of how I actually learned.
Q10: There were many times I was studying from downloaded materials or my own notes without being logged in at all. I'd spend hours reviewing content I'd already accessed once, just listening repeatedly until it made sense. Group study sessions with friends from my extracurriculars also happened offline. The LMS would show nothing for those days even though I was working hard.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd access a video lecture once, download the audio, and then listen to it maybe four or five more times offline. The LMS only captured that initial access. Same with discussion threads - I'd screenshot interesting conversations and review them later on my phone. My actual engagement was way higher than what the system recorded.
Q12: Around midterms everything got really hard. I was feeling sad and my motivation just tanked. Even though I'm usually pretty resilient, the combination of high stress and low mood made it difficult to push through. I'd sit down to study and just feel this heaviness. The CBT sessions helped me recognize these patterns, but in the moment it was still a struggle.
Q13: Definitely. When I was overwhelmed with deadlines from multiple courses, I'd skim through LMS content just to say I'd seen it, not really absorbing anything. Fatigue was a big factor too - I'd log in late at night already exhausted and just go through the motions. My extracurriculars also competed for my energy, and sometimes the course lost that battle.
Q14: More often than I'd like to admit. I'd have videos playing but my mind would be somewhere else entirely, worrying about other things or just feeling too sad to focus. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them. The activity was there but the meaningful learning wasn't. It's frustrating because I knew I was wasting time but couldn't snap out of it.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly even knowing they were important. My assignment completion was really low and I'm not proud of that. Part of it was feeling overwhelmed and not knowing where to start. Part of it was this weird avoidance that comes with feeling sad - the task feels impossible so you just... don't. The CBT helped me understand this pattern but breaking it was another thing entirely.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to submit anything. I'd have bursts of activity right before due dates, cramming and rushing through things. Without a deadline looming, I'd let things slide. The LMS probably shows these spikes clearly. It's not a healthy way to work but when motivation is low, external pressure is sometimes the only thing that moves you.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially positive feedback, it gave me a temporary boost. But negative feedback or poor grades made me want to withdraw even more. After my exam score came back so low, I felt really defeated. Good feedback on discussion posts kept me participating there at least. I needed more encouragement than I got, honestly.
Q18: The course structure with so many components was overwhelming. Having nine courses simultaneously meant I couldn't give this one the attention it probably needed. The weekly modules were clear enough, but the sheer volume of content across all my courses made it hard to keep up. I appreciated the flexibility but also needed more structure to stay on track.
Q19: The video content being available anytime was great for my learning style. But the lack of audio-only options meant I had to be at my computer to watch, which wasn't always possible. Discussion boards being active helped engagement. What made it harder was when deadlines clustered together or when instructions weren't clear about what was actually required versus optional.
Q20: I think that describes me somewhat. I was highly engaged with watching videos and participating in discussions, but my assignment completion was terrible. Sometimes you're genuinely trying to learn but the actual submission part feels like a separate hurdle. You might understand the material but struggle to produce the work, especially when you're dealing with low motivation and sadness.
Q21: That wasn't really my experience since I had high LMS activity. But I can see how someone who learns efficiently offline or has strong prior knowledge might not need to be in the system much. They might download everything once and work independently. The LMS doesn't capture how smart or efficient someone's study methods are outside the platform.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns - when you're active, what you click on, how long videos play. But they miss the quality of that engagement entirely. They can't tell if I was actually listening or just had the video running. They miss all my offline studying, my emotional state, my real understanding. It's like measuring someone's fitness by counting how many times they entered a gym.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and putting genuine mental effort into understanding it. It's not just showing up or clicking through content. For me, engagement is when I'm actively listening, asking myself questions, connecting ideas to things I already know. It's a mental state, not just a behavior that can be tracked.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how hard I was fighting against my own sadness and low motivation just to participate at all. It wouldn't show the hours I spent listening to downloaded lectures while walking around trying to shake off the heaviness I felt. It wouldn't capture that despite my resilience, I was struggling emotionally throughout the course. The data shows what I did, not what it cost me to do it.
